notable.notable_blurb: UNICEF director; accepted 1965 Nobel Peace Prize

cross_connections.display_label: Junior ECA Paris staffer on Marshall Plan, later Fitzgerald biographer
his father-in-law, who shared a Nobel for radioactivity
his mother-in-law, twice a Nobel laureate

narrative_blocks.body: Henry Labouisse became Executive Director of UNICEF in 1965 — the year UNICEF was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. He accepted the prize on the organization's behalf. UNICEF would conduct major relief operations under his fourteen-year leadership, including during the Nigerian civil war in 1968 and in Cambodia in 1979.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Eve Curie; son-in-law of Marie Curie
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on November 19, 1954 to Ève Curie made him son-in-law of Pierre and Marie Curie. The Curie family won five Nobel Prizes — Pierre and Marie (Physics 1903), Marie again (Chemistry 1911), Irène and Frédéric Joliot-Curie (Chemistry 1935), and Henry himself accepting the Peace Prize for UNICEF in 1965. Ève survived him by twenty years, dying in 2007 at age 102.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-great-grandson of Confederate bishop Leonidas Polk
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Frances Devereux Huger Labouisse descended from Leonidas Polk (X00867) — the Episcopal bishop killed as a Confederate general at Pine Mountain in 1864. Polk's daughter Elizabeth Devereux Polk married William Elliott Huger; their daughter Frances was Henry's mother. The Curie marriage thus joined Henry's two genealogical headlines — Hooker-Edwards Puritan lineage on the New England side, Polk Confederate aristocracy on the Louisiana side.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

notable.notable_blurb: UNICEF director; accepted 1965 Nobel Peace Prize

his mother-in-law, twice a Nobel laureate

narrative_blocks.body: Henry Labouisse became Executive Director of UNICEF in 1965 — the year UNICEF was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. He accepted the prize on the organization's behalf. UNICEF would conduct major relief operations under his fourteen-year leadership, including during the Nigerian civil war in 1968 and in Cambodia in 1979.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Eve Curie; son-in-law of Marie Curie
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on November 19, 1954 to Ève Curie made him son-in-law of Pierre and Marie Curie. The Curie family won five Nobel Prizes — Pierre and Marie (Physics 1903), Marie again (Chemistry 1911), Irène and Frédéric Joliot-Curie (Chemistry 1935), and Henry himself accepting the Peace Prize for UNICEF in 1965. Ève survived him by twenty years, dying in 2007 at age 102.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-great-grandson of Confederate bishop Leonidas Polk
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Frances Devereux Huger Labouisse descended from Leonidas Polk (X00867) — the Episcopal bishop killed as a Confederate general at Pine Mountain in 1864. Polk's daughter Elizabeth Devereux Polk married William Elliott Huger; their daughter Frances was Henry's mother. The Curie marriage thus joined Henry's two genealogical headlines — Hooker-Edwards Puritan lineage on the New England side, Polk Confederate aristocracy on the Louisiana side.

narrative_blocks.header: The only Curie without a Nobel Prize
narrative_blocks.body: Ève Curie was the younger daughter of Marie and Pierre Curie, and the only member of her family who did not become a scientist. She joked that she was the only Curie without a Nobel Prize — until 1965, when her husband Henry Labouisse accepted the Nobel Peace Prize on behalf of UNICEF, completing the family legacy of five Nobel laureates.

narrative_blocks.header: Her biography of Marie Curie won the National
narrative_blocks.body: Published in 1937, Ève's biography Madame Curie became an international bestseller and won the National Book Award. She followed it with Journey Among Warriors (1943), dispatches from the front lines of World War II that earned a Pulitzer nomination.

From the 1960s onward Ève devoted herself to UNICEF, traveling to over 100 countries with her husband, the organization's executive director. The United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan visited her in her New York apartment on her 100th birthday in 2004. She died in Manhattan in 2007 at age 102.

notable.notable_blurb: New Orleans cotton merchant; UNICEF Nobel director's father

narrative_blocks.header: New Orleans cotton merchant; raised three Labouisse sons
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Richardson Labouisse Sr. was a New York-born cotton merchant who built his career in New Orleans. He married Frances Devereux Huger in 1895 and raised three sons: William Huger, John Witherspoon, and Henry Richardson Jr. He died at New Orleans in 1930 at 62, before his youngest son's diplomatic ascent and UNICEF Nobel acceptance.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

notable.notable_blurb: Granddaughter of Polk; mother of UNICEF Nobel director

narrative_blocks.header: Her son accepted a Nobel Prize
narrative_blocks.body: Her youngest son, Henry Richardson Labouisse Jr., directed UNICEF and accepted the 1965 Nobel Peace Prize on its behalf — and married Ève Curie, daughter of Marie. Frances herself was a granddaughter of Bishop-General Leonidas Polk, the “fighting bishop” of the Confederacy.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Henry Richardson Labouisse, 1895 New Orleans
narrative_blocks.body: Frances 'Fannie' Huger married Henry Richardson Labouisse Sr. on January 10, 1895 at New Orleans. They raised three sons in Louisiana: William Huger (HD2604), John Witherspoon (HD2605), and Henry Richardson Labouisse Jr. (HD2606). She lived to see Henry Jr. become a US ambassador, dying in Manhattan in 1948 at age 81 before his UNICEF tenure.

sources.short: The Daily Picayune obit, July 4, 1901, page 10
Sons of Confederate Veterans (military service records)

notable.primary_url_label: American Aristocracy (re: father Col. John M. Huger)

marriages.notes: Bidirectional marriage record synced from HD2600 in Stage 6Y-269.

residence.address_note: Funeral from 3645 Coliseum Street, New Orleans (his late residence)

units: Co. B, 1st Special (Rightor's) Battalion, Louisiana Infantry
Co. I, Dreux's Battery, Louisiana Artillery
postwar_associations: Louisiana Division Camp No. 2, United Confederate Veterans (Army of Tennessee Association) — president

narrative_blocks.header: President of the New York Stock Exchange
narrative_blocks.body: William Elliott Huger rose to the presidency of the New York Stock Exchange in the late nineteenth century. He died at age 60 on July 3, 1901 in New York City; his funeral was held from his New Orleans residence at 3645 Coliseum Street. He was buried beside his wife at Metairie Cemetery, New Orleans.

narrative_blocks.header: First Lieutenant, Louisiana Confederate infantry and artillery
narrative_blocks.body: Huger served as 1st Lieutenant in Co. B of the 1st Special (Rightor's) Battalion Louisiana Infantry and in Co. I of Dreux's Battery Louisiana Artillery during the Civil War, plus as aide-de-camp on the General's Staff. He maintained a postwar association with the Louisiana Division Camp No. 2 of the United Confederate Veterans for the rest of his life. He served as president of the Army of Tennessee Association in Louisiana.

narrative_blocks.header: Father a Seminole War and Civil War Colonel
narrative_blocks.body: His father Col. John Middleton Huger was appointed Adjutant General with the rank of Major under Gen. Moore in the brigade of North Alabama troops at the outbreak of the 1835 Seminole War, where he distinguished himself in many acts of bravery. He served as Confederate Colonel under Gen. Braxton Bragg in the Civil War and afterward retired to large sugar plantations on Red River and Côte Blanche Island in Louisiana. Col. Huger died in Manhattan at 537 Fifth Avenue and was a longtime schoolmate and lifelong friend of Hamilton Fish.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandfather a US Senator; great-grandfather a Signer
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Judge Daniel Elliott Huger served as United States Senator from South Carolina in the antebellum period. His great-grandfather on the maternal side was Arthur Middleton — one of South Carolina's four signers of the Declaration of Independence in 1776. The Hugers themselves descended from French Huguenots who emigrated to South Carolina in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

narrative_blocks.header: Married NYSE president William Elliott Huger, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Devereux Polk married William Elliott Huger in 1864 — joining her father Bishop/General Leonidas Polk's Confederate line with the Huger family of South Carolina antebellum public men. The marriage produced six children, including Frances Devereux 'Fannie' Huger Labouisse (HD2603). Through Frances's son Henry Richardson Labouisse Jr., the union would extend to UNICEF's 1965 Nobel Peace Prize and into the Curie scientific dynasty.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
